retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should have two large bore IV lines in place and blood crossed and matched. Uterine artery catheters can be placed before the procedure by interventional radiology for precautions as well. Regional anesthesia, spinal-epidural combination, is recommended at the time of delivery for nonurgent cases. [17] In the event a hysterectomy is necessary, the patient can convert to general anesthesia. [17] [18] Regional anesthesia is considered superior to general anesthesia because of the decreased operative blood loss and the need for blood transfusion. [6] Inhaled anesthetics can lead to uterine relaxation worsening postpartum hemorrhage. During the procedure, if there is a postpartum hemorrhage, then the catheters can be inflated to decrease the blood supply to the uterus.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: A cesarean section should optimally occur under controlled conditions. A discussion with the patient should take place during prenatal care of the diagnosis, possible complications, and the plan for cesarean section and possible hysterectomy if there is uncontrolled postpartum hemorrhage or PAS. The surgeon, anesthesiologist, nursing staff, pediatricians, and blood bank should receive notification of these patients. If there is a concern for PAS then urology, general surgery, as well as interventional radiology should have involvement as well. Communication should take place among the teams regarding the expected date of surgery, planned procedures such as uterine artery embolization, and updated imaging studies, which allows the various units to be aware of the patient if the patient presents earlier in an emergency setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta previa is the complete or partial covering of the internal os of the cervix with the placenta. [1] [2] [3] It is a major risk factor for postpartum hemorrhage and can lead to morbidity and mortality of the mother and neonate. [4] This situation prevents a safe vaginal delivery and requires the delivery of the neonate to be via cesarean delivery. Most cases are diagnosed early on in pregnancy via sonography and others may present to the emergency room with painless vaginal bleeding in the second or third trimester of pregnancy. The presence of placenta previa can also increase a woman's risk for placenta accreta spectrum (PAS). [5] This spectrum of conditions includes placenta accreta, increta, and percreta. Uncontrolled postpartum hemorrhage from placenta previa or PAS may necessitate a blood transfusion, hysterectomy thus leaving the patient infertile, admission to the ICU, or even death.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta previa can lead to serious consequences and requires immediate attention to the presentation of vaginal bleeding. an interprofessional team approach must be taken to provide the utmost care for the patient. Patients diagnosed with placenta previa prior to vaginal bleeding should have multiple discussions with the OB/GYN regarding management and expectations. Patients should consent for possible blood transfusions, uterine artery embolization, and possible cesarean hysterectomy. Various disciplinaries, participating in an interprofessional team approach should be aware of the patient including, labor and delivery physicians, anesthesiologist, interventional radiologist, vascular surgeon, urology surgeon, and both surgical and neonatal nurses.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal bleeding secondary to placenta previa can lead to postpartum hemorrhage requiring a blood transfusion, hysterectomy, maternal intensive care admission, septicemia, and maternal death. [6] Postpartum hemorrhage is blood loss greater than or equal to 1000 ml accompanied by signs or symptoms of hypovolemia occurring within 24 hours after delivery, regardless of the route of delivery. [24] This condition may necessitate blood transfusion, uterotonics, uterine artery embolization, iliac artery ligation, balloon tamponade, and hysterectomy. Placenta previa that is not diagnosed early enough or managed improperly can lead to morbidity and mortality for both the mother and fetus. Placenta previa is also associated with preterm birth, low birth weight, lower APGAR scores, longer duration of hospitalization, and higher blood transfusion rates. [1] Women with placenta previa and prior history of cesarean sections are at an increased risk of PAS. Risk of placenta accreta is 3%, 11%, 40%, 61%, and 67%, for the first, second, third, fourth, and fifth or more cesarean, respectively. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: About 90% of placenta previa cases resolve through delivery. [1] Jing et al. found that women with anterior placentas have poorer prognostic factors and are more likely to have massive blood loss and higher hysterectomy rates compared to any other location. [7] This outcome is secondary to the placenta attaching to a prior uterine incision causing PAS and an incision going through the placenta. Patient's with confirmed placenta previa are at risk for blood transfusion, injury to nearby organs, cesarean hysterectomy (0.2%), intensive care admission, and death. [6] There is also an increased risk in subsequent pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with a low-lying placenta, placenta lies greater than 2cm from the cervical os, may qualify for a trial of labor but are at an increased risk for postpartum hemorrhage and emergent cesarean section compared to women with normal placentation. [6] [21] There is no consensus for the recommended mode of delivery for marginal placenta previa. [6] One study by Jansen et al. showed that if the distance of the edge of the placenta to the internal os is greater than 10mm a trial of labor should be the recommended procedure. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta previa is the complete or partial covering of the internal os of the cervix with the placenta. It is a major risk factor for postpartum hemorrhage and can lead to morbidity and mortality of the mother and neonate. This situation prevents a safe vaginal delivery and requires the delivery of the neonate to be via cesarean delivery. Most cases are diagnosed early on in pregnancy via sonography and others may present to the emergency room with painless vaginal bleeding in the second or third trimester of pregnancy. The presence of placenta previa can also increase a woman's risk for placenta accreta spectrum (PAS). This spectrum of conditions includes placenta accreta, increta, and percreta. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of patients with placenta previa. It also highlights the role of an interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition in order to improve outcomes for mother and fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Accreta -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: PAS disorders are a challenging obstetric condition that mandates coordination and collaboration among interprofessional team members. These teams should include perinatologists to assist with diagnosis, intensivists for postpartum and post-surgical care in the intensive care unit, and neonatologists to care for the preterm infant. Additionally, the provider performing the surgery should be comfortable performing cesarean hysterectomies and have access to surgeons who can assist in the case of percreta or damage to surrounding organs. This may include gynecologic oncologists, general surgeons, or urologists. A well-equipped blood bank should be available for the possibility of a massive transfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta previa is the covering of the internal cervical os preventing a safe vaginal delivery. Delivery should be performed from 36 to 37 full weeks, via cesarean section, in uncomplicated cases. [15] Bed rest, avoiding intercourse, and avoiding digital examinations are recommended. Any manipulation of the placenta can lead to hemorrhage. Patients with placenta previa should go to the emergency room if they have any episodes of vaginal bleeding. Prior history of placenta previa and a history of cesarean sections may increase a patient's risk for placenta accreta. If there is a concern for placenta accreta, increta, or percreta, then a cesarean hysterectomy may need to be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthetic Management of Ex Utero Intrapartum Treatment (EXIT) and Fetal Surgery -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The maternal and fetal complications occur more with open surgeries as compared to minimally invasive surgeries. The incidence of premature rupture of membranes, placental abruption, postoperative vaginal bleeding, preterm delivery, pulmonary edema is similar in endoscopic and open procedures. [16] EXIT procedures have a significantly higher risk of bleeding than other fetal surgeries and are associated with a higher incidence of transfusion. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Previa -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who present with a known history of placenta previa and vaginal bleeding should have vitals performed, and should have electronic fetal monitoring initiated. The patient should receive two large-bore intravenous lines with a complete blood count, type and screen, and have coags drawn. If she presents with substantial bleeding, then 2-4 units of blood should be crossed and matched.

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Accreta -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis is better for patients who have placenta accreta without placenta previa. Placenta accreta with previa has a higher risk of hemorrhage and is more likely to undergo a hysterectomy, both of which contribute to morbidity. [12] Patients who have placenta percreta are at an increased risk of complications compared to placenta accreta and increta. These patients have a statistically significant higher rate of renal tract injuries, intensive care unit (ICU) admissions, and the need for additional blood products. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Placenta Abnormalities -- Issues of Concern -- Placenta Previa
retrieved_docs.content: Placenta previa occurs when the placenta implants totally or partially in the lower segment of the uterus rather than in the fundus. In complete previa, the internal os is completely covered by the placenta. [1] In partial previa, the placenta covers a portion of the internal os. [1] [5] In marginal previa, the edge of the placenta extends to the edge of the cervical os. [1] [5] [1] A low-lying placenta is commonly defined as one within 2 cm of the cervical os without covering any portion. [1] These conditions occur in approximately 1 in 200 to 250 pregnancies, and risk factors include prior cesarean delivery, previous abortion, prior intrauterine surgery, smoking, multifetal gestation, increasing parity, and maternal age. [1] [5] [1] A woman with a history of placenta previa is 12 times more likely to have placenta previa in a subsequent pregnancy. Ultrasound screening programs during first and early second-trimester pregnancies now include placental localization. [1] Transvaginal ultrasound can also make a diagnosis. Delivery should be by Cesarean section as dilation of the cervix causes separation of the placenta, leading to bleeding from the open vessels. [1] [5] Vaginal delivery is an option for those with low-lying placentas as the bleeding morbidity has proven to be limited. [1] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Placental Abruption -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications that can manifest with placental abruption are as follows: Severe hemorrhage Fetal demise Maternal death Delivering premature infant Coagulopathy Transfusion-associated complications Hysterectomy Cesarean section means future deliveries are all via cesarean section Recurrence has been reported in 4% to 12% of cases Increased risk of adverse cardiac events have been reported in women with placental abruption

retrieved_docs.title: Intraoperative and Anesthesia Awareness -- Issues of Concern -- Risk Factors Related to Surgical Procedure or Context
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency surgery, particularly trauma and cesarean section, poses relatively higher risks of AAGA due primarily to the reduction of anesthetic doses that are often required in these situations. [8] [9] In the setting of emergent trauma, blood loss, and subsequent hemodynamic instability often preclude the delivery of anesthetics that are sufficient to prevent awareness and recall, as potent anesthetics are commonly associated with decreases in both myocardial contractility and systemic vascular resistance. Emergent cesarean delivery necessitates a rapid pace from identification of fetal or maternal distress to surgical delivery, resulting in scenarios where adequate anesthetic concentrations may not be achieved at the time of surgical incision. Additionally, anesthetics are often intentionally held during cesarean section to lower concentrations even when the interval between induction and delivery is longer, as anesthetics easily cross the placenta and may result in both respiratory and circulatory depression in the fetus. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: outcome category ولادة جنين ميت (delivery of a dead fetus), indicating that the combined risks of surgical termination, severe hemorrhage, placental abruption, and placenta previa contribute to the possibility of stillbirth. Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how specific amniotic cavity problems, early membrane rupture, cesarean delivery, missed abortion, anesthesia‑related cardiac and central nervous system complications, fetal disorders, and termination‑related hemorrhagic conditions are interlinked with pregnancy complications and their late effects, ultimately influencing the risk of delivering a non‑viable infant and underscoring the importance of careful obstetric monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2073
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY encompasses several serious obstetric conditions, specifically hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three entities are classified as hemorrhagic complications of pregnancy, representing major sources of maternal morbidity and mortality. Recognizing that hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa fall under the broader category of pregnancy‑related complications highlights their interrelated nature and underscores the importance of prompt diagnosis and coordinated management in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, including the specific conditions abruptio placenta and placenta previa, are classified together as types of obstetric hemorrhage. This classification groups these distinct pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders, emphasizing their shared relevance to maternal health and guiding clinical assessment and management of hemorrhagic complications in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: or status of the mother” refine the clinical picture and guide care during the maternal pregnancy period. The hierarchy connects broad categories—“Complications mainly related to pregnancy” and “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium”—to their specific manifestations. Indications for maternal care are organized under the parent concept of previous cesarean section, emphasizing that uterine scarring influences obstetric management decisions. Threatened labor serves as a parent concept for various labor‑related conditions, including false labor, cervical shortening, and cervical insufficiency. Miscarriage, recurrent miscarriage (“إجهاض معتاد”), spontaneous abortion and surgical termination of pregnancy are grouped within safety‑monitoring queries for termination of pregnancy and risk of miscarriage. Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta and placenta previa are members of the standardized MedDRA query “إنهاء الحمل وخطر

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa, underscoring their contribution to the risk of miscarriage and stillbirth. Overall, the knowledge network integrates normal and abnormal labor processes, a spectrum of hemorrhagic and coagulopathic disorders, mechanical pelvic abnormalities, specific obstetric interventions, and their hierarchical relationships, providing a detailed framework for understanding obstetric risk, management, and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1632
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY are classified as having the parent conditions of hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa, indicating that these specific obstetric hemorrhagic events constitute the primary pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 916
retrieved_docs.content: previa without hemorrhage). Although the latter denotes a previa without active bleeding, it is grouped together with hemorrhage in pregnancy to emphasize its close clinical relationship and potential progression to bleeding. Overall, the knowledge base links inverted uterus and various hemorrhagic conditions to the maternal antenatal period, situates hemorrhage in pregnancy within the spectrum of gestational abnormalities and puerperal disorders, and delineates multiple specific hemorrhagic subtypes—ranging from placental previa variants to coagulation‑related parturient hemorrhages—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1019
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa are closely related obstetric complications, each of which can result in significant maternal bleeding. Placenta previa, referred to in Arabic as مشيمة منزاحة, is a specific form of placental displacement in which the placenta partially or completely covers the cervical os, predisposing the patient to bleeding especially as the uterus expands. Abruptio placenta, the premature separation of the placenta from the uterine wall, is another direct cause of hemorrhage during pregnancy. Recognizing that مشيمة منزاحة (placenta previa) is a child concept within the broader category of placental disorders that include abruptio placenta helps clinicians assess risk, monitor for bleeding, and implement appropriate management strategies to protect both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage performed after delivery or abortion (dilatation and curettage, D&C) is classified as a post‑delivery/abortion procedure and is linked to the broader process of الولادة (delivery), which may occur as a normal spontaneous vaginal birth or be complicated by a spectrum of obstetric disorders. Central to obstetric morbidity is النزف في الحمل (bleeding in pregnancy), which serves as the parent category for placental bleeding (نزف في المشيمة) and for numerous hemorrhagic presentations, including placenta previa (مقدمة المشيمة) of any subtype—marginal, partial, or total—with or without intrapartum hemorrhage, parturient hemorrhage related to fibrinogen metabolism disorders (afibrinogenemia, hypofibrinogenemia, hyperfibrinolysis), bleeding during labor (نزف أثناء الولادة), unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and maternal/fetal hemorrhage syndromes. High‑risk pregnancy (حمل عالي الخطورة) is mapped to placenta previa, displaced placenta (مشيمة منزاحة), and to a wide array

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1822
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is directly associated with the placenta, indicating that this structural abnormality of the placental edge involves placental tissue. Both Marginal placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage and Partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage also have the placenta as their primary finding site, reflecting that abnormal placental implantation near or over the cervical os can lead to bleeding during labor. The placenta itself may present in several distinct locations and pathological states: it can be described as “placenta; fenestrata,” denoting a fenestrated or perforated placental surface, and as “مشيمة مزدوجة,” indicating a double placenta configuration. Additional placental findings include “قصور المشيمة” (placental insufficiency), which can compromise fetal nutrient and oxygen delivery; “متلازمة انتقال الدم بطريق المشيمة” (placental transfusion syndrome), a condition where abnormal blood flow through the placenta affects maternal‑fetal circulation; “نزف الأم والجنين” (maternal‑fetal hemorrhage), representing bleeding that involves both mother and fetus; and “نزف في المشيمة” (placental hemorrhage), a localized bleeding event within the placental tissue. Recognizing these interrelated placental conditions and their specific locations or complications is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of maternal and fetal outcomes during pregnancy and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2038
retrieved_docs.content: Previous cesarean section complicating pregnancy or childbirth is recognized as a specific manifestation of a previous c-section, indicating that a prior cesarean delivery can create complications in subsequent pregnancy or childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: preclude proceeding with labor. Collectively, these entities and relationships illustrate the full spectrum of normal and pathological events surrounding delivery, the pivotal role of placental and uterine pathology in maternal‑fetal hemorrhage, and the importance of procedural, positional, and coagulation assessments in optimizing obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2923
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified under the broader category of other conditions or status of the mother that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, and it specifically includes hemorrhagic events occurring during pregnancy such as abruptio placenta and placenta previa. This hierarchical relationship indicates that hemorrhage‑related complications are a subset of the maternal conditions that can adversely affect the gestational and peripartum periods, highlighting the importance of recognizing and managing these specific obstetric hemorrhages within the overall context of pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ, which captures recurrent miscarriage, hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, placenta previa, and other hemorrhagic complications. Overall, the system integrates anatomical, infectious, metabolic, surgical, hemorrhagic, endocrine, neurologic, psychiatric, and hematologic maternal factors within a structured framework, linking individual diagnoses to broader categories of unspecified pregnancy complications, glucose‑tolerance disorders, maternal‑focused complication groups, and standardized safety queries to support comprehensive risk assessment, accurate classification, and coordinated patient‑centered care throughout pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 720
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a broad category that includes several serious obstetric events: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These three conditions are closely linked, as each involves abnormal bleeding or separation of the placenta and can lead to or coexist with a displaced placenta, referred to in Arabic as مشيمة منزاحة. The presence of a مشيمة منزاحة often reflects the underlying pathology of hemorrhagic episodes, premature placental detachment (abruptio placenta), or abnormal placental implantation (placenta previa). Recognizing that Hemorrhage during pregnancy, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa are primary complications that may result in a مشيمة منزاحة helps clinicians prioritize early detection, appropriate monitoring, and timely intervention to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2035
retrieved_docs.content: Circumvallate Placenta Syndrome is a condition that is directly related to the placenta. Both marginal placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage and partial placenta previa with intrapartum hemorrhage are identified as having the placenta as the primary site of pathology. The placenta itself can present in several anatomical configurations, including multipartite (multilobed) and tripartite (three‑lobed) forms, as well as a displaced placenta that occurs without bleeding. Pathological findings associated with the placenta encompass placental insufficiency, fetal‑maternal hemorrhage syndrome, and combined maternal and fetal hemorrhage. Recognizing these interconnections among placental disorders, their locations, and associated hemorrhagic complications is essential for accurate diagnosis and optimal obstetric care.
